topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to vibrate under the fluorescent lights. Sarah stared at the 'D-' with a slow, deliberate motion, her pen hovering an inch above the dotted line. It wasn't just the mark; it was the sudden, cold emptiness that flooded her chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. She squeezed her eyes shut, trying to mentally rewind the last few weeks—the late nights fueled by caffeine, the hours spent wrestling with the calculus problems, the confident assumption that she had mastered the material. When she opened her eyes, the textbook felt heavy, suffocating. A faint tremor ran through her hand as she slowly, mechanically, gathered her things. The hallway seemed impossibly loud, every footstep echoing the sudden, sharp realization that her foundation felt dangerously unstable.

[story 2]

Mark reread the feedback three times, his jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. The grade was a betrayal, a stark, unwelcome indictment of his perceived effort. He traced the words with his fingertip, the texture of the paper rough beneath his touch. A strange, hot prickle started at the base of his neck, spreading outward until his skin felt prickly and tight. He stood up abruptly, knocking his chair slightly askew. "No," he whispered to the empty room, the sound thin and reedy. He walked to the window, the city outside blurring into streaks of indifferent light. He didn't want to look at his notes again; the sheer weight of the disappointment pressed down on him, making his vision swim slightly. He just needed to get out, to escape the suffocating atmosphere of the room.

[story 3]

"A C-minus." The words landed in Liam’s ears with a dull thud, instantly draining the color from his face. He felt a sudden, overwhelming pressure behind his eyes, a dizzying sensation that made the room tilt slightly. He gripped the edge of his desk until his knuckles turned white, the wood digging painfully into his palms. He didn't move for a long moment, just breathing in shallow, quick gasps. When he finally forced himself to look at the score again, the numbers swam, refusing to cohere into anything meaningful. A slow, heavy sigh escaped him, a sound laced with utter defeat. He felt a profound sense of dislocation, as if the ground beneath his feet had suddenly vanished.
